paragraphs.text: having adopted this latter name while I was a sailor. My native place is
. I went to sea while still a boy, making my first voyage with
. I continued to follow the sea, gradually rising from my first position as cooks boy to ablebodied seaman.
At the age of 19 having already followed the sea 2 years I shipped on board an old brigbark in ballast, called
. It was in the month of Feb'y when we left

leaked badly, so that it was necessary to work the pumps day & night continually. The weather was also very cold so that being almost constantly wet, & no clothes with to change, my kit having been stolen on the boarding house in
previous to sailing. I suffered very much. Upon our return to
determined to make no more voyages to cold climates. I next shipped in a vessel for the

. Here I was taken sick with the fever. I then spoke Eng. moderately well & so used no German. In the hospital in where I was taken when I fell sick there were several German nurses. One of them attended the beds among which I lay.

My next neighbor was hopelessly sick with fever & one day this nurse came to give him his medicine. As he did so he siad [said] in German, not knowing that any one near could understand him, "that will be the last of you poor fellow." I got out of that Hospital just as soon as I could. I soon recovered & returned to
. Here I shipped again on an old brig called the "
however she was chartered & instead of going back to
. She was loaded with an assorted cargo & was to call at so many ports on

& remain so long in each place. They came on board bringing most of the crew of their own vessel which they had just sold. Here we got caught in a severe Norther, & our 1st Mate in trying to secure a gun on deck during the gale had his foot crushed so badly that he died in the Hospital before we left. Capt Wilson’s Mate took his place. From
. While lying here we were all givingen liberty & one dollar each to spend. One of our crew, a Scotchman by the name of
, remained ofover his time so that a short time after when we were again given liberty he was not allowed to go on shore. The next time he did get ashore he went at once to the Eng. Consul & reported
& told him of the report & informing that unless he could induce
to withdraw the complaint he would have to inspect
& condemn her if found as reported.
, our capt, returned soon after with Consul & assured him that he had made everything all right with the men, although he had in reality said nothing to us.
, was not to be foiled in this manner. After waiting a few days he suspected that

had put a stop to the investigation which the Consul was in duty bound to make, in view of the complains. He went a second time & several of us accompanied him. He asked the Consul why his complains had been neglected & upon learning the action of
, he repeated his complains in which we all joined him & we also declared that no understanding had been arrived at between
& the crew. The next day two Capts were sent down, & after a careful examination they pronounced he [her] totally unseaworthy, whereupon the Consul condemned her. As charterers Capt's
were required to have her repaired & not a pound of her cargo could they remove until such repairs were completed. They took advantage of

who was a drunken character, & while under the influence of liquor persuaded him to sign a paper relieving them of all responsibility. This accomplished the [they] took their cargo out of the vessel & transferred it to

which they bought here. The crew were all discharged here & received certificates of the ships indebtedness to them but they could obtain no money until it should came from the Old Country. Three of us shipped on
, the rest of the crew being made up from the men
had brought with them, among whom were several
. Arrived here the goods were all landed & placed in the
. The duties at that time were very heavy.
As soon as we got got the cargo all on shore, a
& I were sent to watch it with the mate. This was what is known as a "soft billet" among the sailors
The trust & confidence shown me by

aroused the jealousy of the boatswain who tried by false reports to influence them against me, as he thought he should have been appointed to the light & agreeable task of watching the cargo. Soon after our arrival here Scott & Wilson's mate called
where he went by the first ship. From
having reshipped our goods we came direct to
Here the goods were again landed & I was detailed to superintend their transportation in ox-carts from the beach to the store-house which was the old adobe building that one still standing just below
. Here I remained all the summer of 1844 in charge of the store house while
was trading alongup & down the coast. The boatswain was discharged here & again tried to influence
against me by false reports. This time I gave a good clubbing, very near breaking his arm. Soon after this affair I asked for & received my discharge, & began to go with
. I made my first trip with
, about April of 1845. Besides myself there were in the crew three (3)four (4)
, 3 of them steersman & a cook; an American sailor by the name of
, which after landing us with boats, supplies & materials for building a large boat proceeded to
where she was shortly sold. The first month little else was done but fix up camp & hunt a few wild goats. At the end of this time
started off with their boats crews for the N. side of
to get the timber in shape for the boat. They were gone some to or 720 days & returned with only 6 or 7 otter the weather being too rough for hunting. About a week later
fitted up their boats & went over to
& the rest of the men remained to assist in putting the boat together

came back bringing with them 27 otter skins, many of them undried, having been unable to find sticks with which to stake them out. From 6 to 7 days after their arrival, the boat being completed, we hid some of our supplies on
. Our new boat was of 4 or 5 tons burthen schn. rigged & a good sailer in a free wind, but she had no keel. At
we all went ashore, camped & remained nearly a month, killing about 58 otter more, making in all about 85 otter that we got here. I had charge of the new boat & made several trips to the
for water there being none where we camped. Upon leaving
, where we stopped for our cache. A storm delayedRough weather kept us here over a week. We hunted some on our way up so that we arrived at
with something like 102 otterskins. We got home sometime in Aug.
& I were paid $16 per month; the
fitted out for a trip up
. I had charge of our boat, a large surf boat in which we carried our supplies. The boat we built the year before at
he having furnished the material with which to build it. It was afterwards used in running between
. Our crew this trip were all
sometime in July of 1846 & found the Americans already in possession of it. Here we learned for the first time of the war with
at several placed we found all the ranches deserted & were therefore unable to get any news. Having taken in additional supplies at
, hunting whenever otter were to be found or lying bye in rough weather. In course of time we landedarrived at
the mouth of the Petaluma creek

. Here they were seen by some manofwar officers, who recognizing tham as strangers asked them where they had come from. They told them whereupon two of the officers came over to our camp. One of them was acquainted with
. Finding that we had no passport he advised
, without delay & get one, as we were liable to arrest and detention if taken without one. Accordingly that afternoon
& I with another man took a small boat & went across
was lying; arriving there the next morning Our passport obtained we started to return. There was a very strong wind blowing &
was covered with white caps. Nothing daunted we prepared to go,

having asked the Boatswain's advice as to the best course to take, this individual advised us not to make the attempt to cross, assuring us that he would not risk his life in such a boat, in such weather. He said however that if we were determined to go that it would be best to pull up some distances before starting across. The

. We took his advice & although the manofwar sailors predicted a disaster & watched us as far as they could see, we arrived safely at camp, in a very short time. We got soaking wet & were tossed about like a feather. Just on the right of the
we found a large number of otter. They had not been hunted for years this place having been always claimed by
, &who had forbidden all persons from killing them. We killed about 20 of them & then continued up
until we reached a point about 50 miles above
. Here we got windbound & besides found the shore very high & rocky. From here we returned to
we got wind bound again & while here
while out hunting got caught & severely bitten with a bear. (He tells substantially the same story as
where we took in a few supplies. Thence to
, hunting as opportunity offered on the way. At
we went into camp on the point just above

. While here we were visited by officers from several menofwar then at anchor in the harbor. They bought a few skins from us. At a target match that was gotten up from among Fremont’s men to show the Naval officers some good shooting our Capt.
was asked to join them & he did better shooting than all of them. From
, to just above the border of
having hunted a few otter at
. We landed late in the afternoon in front of a small ravine that opened out on the beach. Here we got supper. Shortly after our arrival
one of our old hunters & companions & who was then living on the "
" nearby came down to warn us against a party of Totoi Pica's men that was coming over that night toto attack us. After supper
asked us if we wanted to pull all night again as we had done the previous night or, stand & fight we said fight.

[sic] with two of the boats where we had landed we put our skins into a third boat & went around a point just below & landed near a high bluff. We buried our extra powder & balls in the sand so then they could not get it, & determined if compelled to retreat to put off with the skins which we knew they were anxious to capture. Our position under the high bluff aforementioned enabled us to fire on anyone above while it was almost impossible, or at least difficult for them to fire on us, or see us in the dark. We drew lots —with three pieces of straw, two short & one long, to decide who should stand guard first.
drew the long one much to his dissatisfaction as he preferred the forepart of the night for sleeping.
& I laid down but found it impossible to sleep, & so began talking about how we would fix the Californians if they attacked us. This annoyed
, who said that if we did not care to sleep that we had better get up & stand guard, & he would lie down. We told him that we were agreeable & so he laid down.
He did not lay long as he too found it impossible to sleep, so after tossing about awhile he proposed that we should continue our journey down
. As he was the Capt, we had nothing to say we. Accordingly we went back to where we had left

[sic] & the boats, taking the ammunition we had buried, & everything being ready we put off. It must have been about midnight. We afterwards heard from some of the same party who came out to take us then they got to our camp shortly after we left as the fire was still burning. There were aboutseveral of them headed by an Italian called
, a regular desperado, who was killed at

two or three days afterwards. They acknowledged that they intended to kill us all & take our skins. They thought we were frightened away at their approach, as one of their men who wanted to smoke a cigarette, struck a light just before they got near our camp. They were sure they would had caught us all if it had not been for this light. We continued to pull all the rest of that night & the next day. The next night we also continued our journey. By this time we were well nigh worn out. Shortly after dark we tied our boats all together & let them drift, all laying down with the intentions of getting rest & sleep.
As on the previous nights I found it utterly impossible to sleep, but this time it was caused by hunger. Accordingly I got up & began searching for something to eat.

heard me & asked from his boat if I had not been able to sleep. I told him that I had not, whereupon he said that he too had found it impossible to sleep. He then said that we had better unfasten our boats. This was hardly accomplished when a squall of wind and water struck us that came very near upsetting our boats. It was so dark that we could not see anything a boat’s length off. I had a compass in my boat but I could not see the needle.
The wind blew the water over us in such a heavy spray that we supposed it a heavy rain at first. We could not determine from which direction the wind was blowing but supposed in afrom the South East.
wanted to put out to sea & actually started but we finally induced him to return & adopt our course of running before the wind. Just before the wind struck us we had proposed to land on
which we supposed must be not far away, & make some coffee & cook something, as since leaving
harbor the night before we had tasted no warm food.
Having failed in this I supposed the other boats would stop & land South of

. I had as also the others now been three nights without sleep. I was steering my boat & long before daylight I found it difficult & even impossible at times to keep my eyes open. In spite of me my eyes would close & I would get sound asleep, & only awaken when the tiller would hit me with force in the ribs. At daylight I rounded

, where the sea was perfectly smooth. I saw that none of the other boats had landed & standing up in my boat, I discovered them far ahead. I at once put on all sail and overtook them before they reached
. When we reached the shore we found six men (Californians) waiting for us, who barely gave us time to land, before they arrested us.
& I were at once taken up to Raymundo Carrillo's house & there asked our nationality.

, being a Scotchman, & I a German we were allowed to go, with the instruction that if we wished to leave town we must get a passport. We told them that we had no desire to leave the place, but purposed making our stay here during the winter has had been our wont.
was told that he would have to go to
a prisoner, as the other Americans had gone. He said he wished to remain here with
, & did not want to be disturbed. They told him that he must go to
since he was an American & that if he would not go alone he should be sent under guard. He promised them that he would go alone, but instead of going he went out into the foot hills near
came, & then joined him. About the same time that
, several Americans were arrested & taken down there & among them
. He hired me to take charge of his house, which I did until
came out about a month after.
came here with his man he went into camp back of the present site of

. He had about 200 men I should think some of them very wild characters. I saw two of them shooting at a target one day. The distance was about 70 or 80 yds. The sunsun shone on the target, so one of them took a mochila & shaded the target standing within a few feet of it while the others shot at it. While here too, two of them got into a row when one seized an axe & would have split the others head open if he had not been prevented.
here. A hunter by the name of
who went up with us & joined
, tried to get me to join here but I did not care to. This winter I remained in
, until April of 1847 when I went overland to
we built 3 boats, there being no lumber for that purpose in
. Having finished our boats we went over to
, to keep our men from getting drunk & leaving us. Our crew not being complete
to get 1 or 2 men, & on the trip lost his boat. Fortunately he had another one still serviceable although old, which he had left in
. We found no otter except at
where we got a doz. perhaps. From
where we refitted & thence continued down
owing to his taking my boat which was one of the new ones for his& and compelling me to take his old one, which leaked. Upon our arrival in
in Aug. (of 1847) I left them. Here they refitted about 2 weeks later & with
This winter I remained on shore & cultivated a garden for a few months, but finding that it would not pay abandoned it. In 1848 I hunted deer, for their skins, on the
. In the fall of this year the
made their last raid on the stock of the Ranchos in this section.
, a Canadian was the majordomo of
& he and I were the only white men there, although there were several
. I noticed several strange horses on
, while out one day & reported it to
. We went out together & found 12 or 15 horses of different brands & we also struck the trail of a number of Indians. Upon questioning the
they confessed that there had been a number of
there who had spoken with them & had told them that they intended to kill
& me but were afraid of our guns. We lost no time in sending word into
to the authorities but before they could send a party out the
[sic] were out of their reach & after an unsuccessful pursuit they took the horses that were identified by their owners & returned to
While hunting deer I averaged about $30 per month. In Aug. of this year

, on foot, & without any boat, to hunt otter. We killed one but the beach being rocky & the surf bad at that point we did not dare to swim out for it & so it was floated away & lost. This so disgusteddiscouraged
that we turned back. On the way we met some parties from the mines who showed us a nugget of gold, & this decided us to try our fortune at
. We fitted out for our trip & were about to start when
fell sick & the trip was abandoned for the time being at least. In March of 1849,
having recovered from his sickness we started from here taking with us two Inds. for the
about two months when I returned to
, bringing as the result of my labor $1,030 in gold. I had returned with the intention of taking several

[sic] back to work for me. Before I was ready I was taken sick, & when I recovered I was persuaded to remain here a short time & help kill some cattle. While engaged in this all of my horses was stolen, & it was not until the Spring of 1850 that I again found it possible to return to
. This time I took but one indian with me. We went to
but after 4 mos. work succeedinged in getting only $500. Having returned to
I worked at different things until April 1851, when
& I with one indian & a single boat made a hunt up
. We found it impossible to run otter with one boat so we returned, having made a very poor hunt, & gone about 2 months. Soon after my return I began building a boat with
. We built it in just 6 days, & gave it the name of
, launching it on that day. We made a short trip to
. There are no otter around the
unless it is an occasional stray one that has been driven away from the schools by the old bulls.
& I, together with our inds. were gone about 2 months & returned with 34 otter skins. The following Winter & Spring I remained on shore.
was employed for a short time in piloting a
. The next Spring he went up to
, bought a schn, brought down lumber & soon after built the house he now lives in, remaining on shore that year. This year I too, staid ashore.
In April of 1853 I went down to
, in the latter’s schn. We went to
where we expected to find gold.
&an old whaling capt, & then capt. of this port, having assured
that his sailors had found gold quartz there. From
, & in overhauling the schn, we found a large hole in her bottom, & the rudder hanging by one pin. From here we went to
. At this latter place we went on shore & remained a week or two, during which time I shot one otter off shore. While here too, Capt’s
, where they had been painting their large boat. We had one hunt together but got nothing, after which we parted, they proceeding to
an English sailor, & two indians [sic] besides
went up in the mts. in search of gold. I remained in charge of the boats lest the men should leave with them.
& I caught several turtles here & boiled them, so that we had turtle meat most of the way back to
returned without finding any gold, whereupon we set sail on our return At
againjoined us & kept us company until we got toat
, where we hunted together, securing 2 or 3 otter. Thence we came direct to
I forgot to mention that in the Fall of 1853 I made a trip to the
& I with two indians [sic] of our crew went ashore to see where the otter lay. At the lower end we discovered quite a school of them. Two days after we again landed about the middle of
& followed along the bank towards its head.
We kept a sharp lookout for signs of
having requested us to search for her. Within about 1/2 mile from head of
we struck a wide low sandy flat that extended from one side of

to the other. Here among some bushes or small trees called by the Californians Malva Real, placed in a crotch or fork of one of the trees we found a basket woven of grass, oblong in shape, 3 ft. long, 1 1/2 ft. wide & about 14 in. deep & covered with a piece of seal skin. Its contents we found were a dress or gown made of the skin of the shag, several skins of the same bird cut in a square shape, bone needles, & knives, fish-hooks made of the abalone shell, & a rope made of sinews, about 1/2 in. in diameter & fully 25 ft. long. This sinew rope was twisted as evenly as the best rope I have ever seen. I think that it was used in snaring seals, by making a noose & spreading it on the rocks near the beach where the seals were accustomed to sleep. The things were all nicely laid together in the basket when we found them, & after examining them I was disposed to replace them & return the basket to the place we had found it.
however, thought that it would be better to leave the things strewn about on the ground, so that on our next visit to this place we might be sure she was still alive & on

if they were found replaced in the basket. Accordingly we left them scattered about. We returned to the vessel intending to make further search as soon as we could find time, but the next two or three days were taken up in hunting otter, & the fourth day &a strong wind sprang up that soon increased to a gale. We remained nearly 6 or 7 days in hopes that the blow would stop, but at the end of that time there being no signs of it going down & the swell becoming so heavy we did not deem it safe to remain longer. Accordingly we ran across to the
, where we found a better harbor than even that of
became somewhat excited & very nearly ran us on a reef.
, where I remained the following winter,
We returned from our trip to
the latter part of June 1853 & a few days after refitted for a hunt among
, as at this season we were not likely to be driven away by a storm as on our previous trip. Then too, there were a large number of otter around
there having been no hunters there for several years, & we wished to secure them before others should go there. We went first to
where we arrived early in the afternoon. We came to anchor on the N.E. side about the middle of
. As soon as we came to anchor
& I went on shore for the purpose of seeing where the otter lay & also to pick out a site for our camp as we intended to make a long stay & would move on shore the next day. We took two of
[sic] with us & left them in charge of the boat while
& I proceeded towards the head of
On the way we saw several dogs that looked very much like a coyote excepting in color which was black & white, they were quite wild & ran away as soon as we came in sight. Arrived at the head of
said that he was tired & sat down to rest. I continued along near the shore however, rounded the point at the head of

& went a long distance down on the other side. Not far from the head I againfound footprints leading from the beach up over the bank & thence up on to the ridge above. I followed them until they disappeared in a kind of moss with which the ground was covered, a short distance above the bank. As it was growing late I returned to where I had left
& reported what I had seen He said at first that they might be the tracks of one of
[sic], but, he afterwards admitted that it was impossible for them to have got ahead of us, & I too was sure that the footprints were too small for those of a man.

however was not very sanguine about finding her as he had come to the conclusion that the dogs had eaten her & was very doubtful if even her bones could be found. He decided however to make a thorough examination of the upper end of
especially the high ridge which lay between the low sandy flat & the head of
. Accordingly after breakfast the next day, we took
& proceeded to the low sandy flat aforementioned. Here I took them, 3
, & stretched them in a line across this end of the flat, we passed across to the other side, leaving
to follow along near the shore to the head of
over the same course he & I had taken the evening before. We reached the other side of
back with instructions to search thoroughly along both borders of the flat & especially to visit the bushes where we had found the basket on our previous visit to
. I did not return with them but went up that side of
until I struck the footprints I had discovered the day before.

I followed the tracks up over the bank & from the point where they could no longer be seen continued to ascend the ridge. About half way up I found a small piece of drift wood which I concluded she must have dropped on her way from the beach with fire wood. From where I found the piece of drift wood I could see three huts farther up the ridge & having gone up to them I found them constructed of whales ribs & covered with brush although they were open all round & the high grass growing within them showed that they could not have been occupied for some time From this point I could seelook over the whole length & breadth of the ridge & the sand flat beyond where I could plainly see our men moving about.

I began to look about me & finally discovered at a distance on the N.E. side of the ridge & about halfway to its top, a small black object that from where I stood looked like a crow seated on a bush. I thought I saw it move, & so went towards it. I soon discovered that it was

. She was seated within an inclosure similar to those already described (see Nidever's account) so that until quite near her I could see only her head & shoulders. I approached her cautiously & was enabled to get within a few yards of her unobserved as she had her face turned from the direction in which I had came. While I was still some distance away two dogs, probably the same we had seen the day before began to growl whereupon she gave a yell & they went away; but she did not turn around. From this point I could plainly see

searching about on the sandy flat, & I signaled to them by placing my hat on the ramrod of my gun & raising & lowering it until I succeeded in attracting their attention, when I made signs for them to come.
saw them also, as every few minutes she would look towards the flat, shading her eyes from the sun with her hand & talking rapidly to herself. While
were coming towards me I had an opportunity of observing

& her surroundings. She was seated cross legged on the ground & was engaged in separating the blubber from a piece of seal skin which was lying across one knee & held by one hand. In the other hand she grasped a rude knife, a piece of iron hoop thrust into a rough piece of wood for a handle & held so that the back of the hand was turned down, scraping & cutting from instead of towards her. Just outside of the enclosure there was a high pile of ashes & bones showing that she had lived in this place some time. Stone mortars & earthen vessels,Baskets of grass & vessels of the same material made in the shape of a flagoon & lined with asphaltum, used to hold water, where scattered about. On a sinew rope stretched between two poles, several feet above the ground where hanging pieces of seals blubber, while near her was the head of a seal from which the brains, already putrid were running. Her covering consisted of a single garment of the shags' skin, the feathers out & pointing downward, in a shape resembling a loose gown. It was sleeveless, low in the neck & was girded at the waist with a sinew rope. When she stood up, as I afterwards observed, it extended nearly to her ankles. She had no covering on her head; her hair which was thickly matted, & bleached to a reddish brown, hung down to her shoulders.

came near enough I made signs for them to spread out, & approach her in a circle, lest she should attempt to escape. While they were still some distance away, but sufficiently near to prevent her escape,

I stepped around in front of her, but instead of seeing her startled & alarmed, I was surprised to have her bow & smile, as though it was a delight to see me & my visit an every day occurrence. She began a rapid talking & gesticulating, all of which was wholly unintelligible to me. As fast as
approached her she also bowed smiled & talked to them.
They all sat dawn in a circle around her while I made signals to
who was in sight to come to us. After some delay he came up & we sat down with

. Taking some roots from two bags or sacks made of grass she placed them in the coals & as soon as they were roasted she passed them around making motions for us to eat. One of the roots was what is commonly called corcomite among the Californians; the other I do not know the name of.
[sic] among our men tried to talk with
but did not succeed in making themselves understood, neither could they comprehend her language.
could be taken by force if necessary. They replied that there would be no difficulty. Hearing this I told

I did not think there would be any necessity of using force, & that if she could be made to understand what was wanted that she would willingly go with us. Patting her on the shoulder to attract her attention I went through the motions of packing her things in to the baskets, placing this on my back & walking off in the direction of the beach, & then said vamos the sch. for let us go. The motions she no doubt understood but the word vamos seemed to be more intelligible, as upon hearing it her face brightened up & she set to work with alacrity to get ready. She filled her baskets, & in the larger one she place[d] the seal’s head after replacing the putrid brains & tearing away from it bits of adhering flesh. This basket she raised to her back & secured itwith straps passing over her shoulders & under her arms. She took other articles in her hands & started off towards the beach with a load that seemed heavy enough for [a] mule. Two of

& I brought up the rear to guard against any attempt at escape, although no such precaution was necessary. Upon reaching the beach we stopped at a spring that forms a little pool of water under a sort of mound of rocks, & situated but a few yards from the beach. One of the peculiarities of this spring was its surface, which is a[t] all times ruffled with a cool breeze which seems to be continually playing over the pool. I noticed it on this occasion & at several times afterwards. The water was invariably clear & cool. Its source I should judge must have been high up in the ridge. Around this spring were several poles erected & on these we hung the things we had brought from the old woman’s place, for each one of us had our hands full & made motions for
to do the same. We hung the things up very carefully &

followed our example, without, to all appearances, the least reluctance. In the cracks & fissures of the rocks that formed the mound we found thrust numbers of bones which we afterwards came to the conclusion had been placed there by

, to furnish her with food in times of need. I afterwards noticed that she always saved the bones contained in her food, placing them in her baskets, to be taken out at intervals & sucked until they were cleaned of every particle of meat. She also saved the scraps of food that were left & ate them when she felt hungry. She ate very little at a time, but took food several times during the day.
From this spring we proceeded along the beach or rather on top of the bank until we arrived at a path that lead down to another spring on the beach.
who were still ahead continued along the bank but
went down the path to the spring. We saw she intended to wash herself, & so withdrew & waited until she returned when we continued along the bank to
. We made motions for her to get into

, which she did with out any hesitation, & crawled forward to the bow & there knelt down, holding on to the side with her hands. Arrived on board she crept up to the galley’s stove which was on deck, & made signs that it was warm there. We had dinner as soon as we got on board & gave

some of our food. She ate heartily & with an apparent relish & our food at this & and in fact at all times seemed to agree with her That afternoon I busied myself on making a petticoat or skirt for her, out of ticking, & this with a mans shirt, a black necktie & an old cape or cloak that
gave her completed the dress she afterwards wore while with us on
. The following day we went on shore & put up a tent or shelter near the beach at a point already selected. Nearby we made a species of hut for
, who seemed perfectly contented with us making no attempt to leave us, although the opportunity was not wanting. Here we remained about a month. The

[sic], the rest of us being away after otter the greater part of each day. The old woman’s chief occupation was working on her baskets, of which she had several not yet completed, roamwandering about on
She was always anxious to help when she saw an opportunity of making herself useful. She was always cheerful, & always talking & laughing.

She took readily to our food & only on one occasion showed any disposition to return to her former food. I killed an otter from shore one morning, & after hauling it ashore and skinning it were about to throw the carcass in the water, to prevent it becoming offensive near camp. It had been dragged down to the edge of the water & was about being thrown in when

came running down to where it lay, talking & gesticulating excitedly, caught the otter by the flipper & hauled it back to where it had been skinned. Then she made signs to us that, that was an inexcusable waste of meat. To humor her we let it remain where she had dragged whenuntil it began to smell so strong that it was found necessary to remove the carcass. This time she made no objections, but on the contrary made signs that we should take it away, at the same time, to show her disgust she held her nose & made us understand that that meat was bad, & then pointing to ours then& smacking her lips that she liked that better. The otter was a female & was with young, which she would have given birth to in a few days. We took the young one & after skinning it carefully, stuffed the skin & gave it to

. She at once hung it by a string to the roof of her hut, & lying on her back under it would amuse herself for hours at a time by swinging it backwards & forwards. About a month later having secured something over 80 otter skins, & the otter having scattered we started on our return. We had barely left
when a gale sprang up so violent that several times we thought that we should have to return to
. Late in the afternoon however we arrived in safety under the lee of the
When the gale sprang up after leaving

made signs to us that she was going to stop the wind. Accordingly she got down on her knees & remained so for some time apparently engaged in prayer, & facing in the direction of the wind. This she repeated several times during the day until the storm abated late in the afternoon, when turning to us she made signs that her prayers had been answered.

she saw, evidently for the first time, an ox cart & a man on horse back. At first sight of them he[r] delight was unbounded. She laughed & danced, & continued to point at them & talk about them as long as they were in sight.
From the beach we took her to Nidever[']s house where she remained until her death which took place about seven weeks from the time she landed.

sent for indians [sic] from different points in the hopes of finding some one among them who could converse with her but as far as I could learn were unsuccessful. The same day she arrived & for weeks afterwards the house was crowded with people that had come to get a sight at her & her things which we had brought from

She often sang & danced for them; putting on her dress of bird skins. Visitors made her presents of trinkets & sometimes of money but she placed no value on one or the other, unless it be the pleasure she felt in dividing them among the children of Nidever[']s family.
Several proposals were made us by parties to take her to
on exhibition, but we rejected them all, having become too much attached to her to consent to such an arrangement. Nidever[']s family took good care of
but I think they allowed her to eat freely of fruit & vegetables. About a month after we brought her ashore
, who I understood sent them to
